items.passage: A supply chain is the connected network of organizations, people, and processes that moves a product from raw material to final customer. Each link in this network performs a distinct task, such as sourcing, manufacturing, warehousing, or delivery, and the output of one stage becomes the input of the next. Coordinating these stages across separate firms that pursue their own goals is the central management challenge, because no single owner controls the whole chain. [■] When demand information is shared poorly between links, small changes at the retail end can grow into large swings further back. This effect, known as the bullwhip effect, causes factories to over-produce one month and sit idle the next. [■] Managers therefore invest heavily in shared forecasting systems and real-time data. [■] Modern firms increasingly track shipments through digital platforms that update every party at once, reducing guesswork and delay. [■] Such visibility lets a distant supplier adjust output before a shortage spreads. However, even well-coordinated chains remain vulnerable to disruptions, such as port closures or sudden material scarcity, that no forecast can fully anticipate, and resilience may matter as much as efficiency in the years ahead.
paragraphs: A supply chain is the connected network of organizations, people, and processes that moves a product from raw material to final customer. Each link in this network performs a distinct task, such as sourcing, manufacturing, warehousing, or delivery, and the output of one stage becomes the input of the next. Coordinating these stages across separate firms that pursue their own goals is the central management challenge, because no single owner controls the whole chain.

[■] When demand information is shared poorly between links, small changes at the retail end can grow into large swings further back. This effect, known as the bullwhip effect, causes factories to over-produce one month and sit idle the next. [■] Managers therefore invest heavily in shared forecasting systems and real-time data.

[■] Modern firms increasingly track shipments through digital platforms that update every party at once, reducing guesswork and delay. [■] Such visibility lets a distant supplier adjust output before a shortage spreads. However, even well-coordinated chains remain vulnerable to disruptions, such as port closures or sudden material scarcity, that no forecast can fully anticipate, and resilience may matter as much as efficiency in the years ahead.

questions.stem: The word "swings" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: warnings
options.B: delays
options.C: supplies
options.D: fluctuations

questions.explanation: The passage contrasts "small changes" growing into "large swings," and later describes factories over-producing then sitting idle, so "swings" means fluctuations.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how are the stages of a supply chain connected?

options.A: Every stage reports directly to a single central manager.
options.B: Each stage competes with the others for raw materials.
options.C: What one stage produces serves as the material for the next.
options.D: All stages perform the same task at the same time.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that "the output of one stage becomes the input of the next," which option C paraphrases.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the bullwhip effect?

options.A: It chiefly harms retailers rather than factories.
options.B: It can be eliminated entirely with enough effort.
options.C: It grows worse as information moves away from customers.
options.D: It only appears in very large companies.

questions.explanation: Because small retail-end changes "grow into large swings further back," the distortion increases with distance from the customer.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention factories that "over-produce one month and sit idle the next" in paragraph 2?

options.A: To illustrate the concrete cost of the bullwhip effect
options.B: To argue that factories should produce less overall
options.C: To show that forecasting systems always fail
options.D: To describe a typical manufacturing schedule

questions.explanation: The example follows the naming of the bullwhip effect and makes its damage tangible, illustrating its real cost.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because each link sees only its own orders, errors are easy to miss until they have already spread.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates on why poor information sharing distorts demand, fitting after the bullwhip effect is named at slot 2 and before managers' response is described.

items.passage: Quorum sensing is a form of chemical communication by which bacteria measure their own population density and coordinate behavior accordingly. Individual cells continuously release small signaling molecules called autoinducers into their surroundings. As the population grows, the concentration of these molecules rises in step with cell number. [■] Once the concentration crosses a threshold, the molecules bind to receptors and switch on a shared set of genes. This mechanism lets a colony act almost as a single organism. [■] Genes activated this way often control behaviors that are useless for a lone cell but powerful for a crowd, such as producing light, forming protective films, or releasing toxins. [■] By delaying such actions until enough cells are present, bacteria avoid wasting resources on responses that would fail at low numbers. [■] The significance of this system extends to medicine, since many infections depend on quorum-controlled toxins. Researchers are therefore designing molecules that block autoinducer receptors, hoping to disarm bacteria without killing them. However, because the signaling pathways differ across species, a single blocking strategy may prove difficult to generalize, and clinical success remains uncertain.
paragraphs: Quorum sensing is a form of chemical communication by which bacteria measure their own population density and coordinate behavior accordingly. Individual cells continuously release small signaling molecules called autoinducers into their surroundings. As the population grows, the concentration of these molecules rises in step with cell number.

[■] Once the concentration crosses a threshold, the molecules bind to receptors and switch on a shared set of genes. This mechanism lets a colony act almost as a single organism. [■] Genes activated this way often control behaviors that are useless for a lone cell but powerful for a crowd, such as producing light, forming protective films, or releasing toxins. [■]

By delaying such actions until enough cells are present, bacteria avoid wasting resources on responses that would fail at low numbers. [■] The significance of this system extends to medicine, since many infections depend on quorum-controlled toxins. Researchers are therefore designing molecules that block autoinducer receptors, hoping to disarm bacteria without killing them. However, because the signaling pathways differ across species, a single blocking strategy may prove difficult to generalize, and clinical success remains uncertain.

questions.stem: The word "threshold" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: barrier
options.B: average
options.C: container
options.D: limit

questions.explanation: The molecules act once their concentration "crosses" a certain level, so "threshold" means a limit that must be passed.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how do bacteria gauge their population density?

options.A: By sensing the concentration of molecules they release
options.B: By directly counting neighboring cells one by one
options.C: By measuring the temperature of their surroundings
options.D: By comparing their size with that of other cells

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 explains that autoinducer concentration "rises in step with cell number," so density is read from molecule levels.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of quorum sensing EXCEPT:

options.A: It can trigger the release of toxins.
options.B: It relies on molecules that bind to receptors.
options.C: It works identically in every bacterial species.
options.D: It allows a colony to behave like one organism.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "signaling pathways differ across species," contradicting option C; the others are all supported.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about blocking autoinducer receptors?

options.A: It would make bacteria reproduce faster.
options.B: It has already cured most bacterial infections.
options.C: It aims to weaken bacteria rather than destroy them.
options.D: It works only on bacteria that produce light.

questions.explanation: The passage says researchers hope to "disarm bacteria without killing them," implying the goal is to weaken rather than destroy them.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, a single glowing bacterium would be invisible, but a dense colony can light up an entire patch of seawater.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The example illustrates a behavior "powerful for a crowd" like producing light, fitting at slot 2 right after that idea is introduced.

items.passage: An ice age is an extended interval during which large ice sheets cover significant portions of the Earth's surface for thousands of years. Within such an interval, the climate does not stay uniformly cold but alternates between colder phases, when ice advances, and milder phases, when it retreats. These shifts are driven largely by slow, regular changes in the geometry of Earth's orbit and the tilt of its axis. [■] As the planet's tilt and path around the Sun vary, the amount of sunlight reaching high latitudes in summer rises and falls. When summers stay cool, winter snow survives instead of melting, and over centuries it compresses into ice. [■] This buried record allows scientists to read past temperatures from the chemistry of trapped air. [■] Cores drilled from thick ice contain bubbles of ancient atmosphere that preserve the gases present when the snow fell. [■] By comparing these layers, researchers reconstruct climate cycles stretching back hundreds of thousands of years. However, the orbital changes alone are too small to explain the full temperature swings, so feedbacks such as shifting ice cover may amplify them, and the complete mechanism remains debated.
paragraphs: An ice age is an extended interval during which large ice sheets cover significant portions of the Earth's surface for thousands of years. Within such an interval, the climate does not stay uniformly cold but alternates between colder phases, when ice advances, and milder phases, when it retreats. These shifts are driven largely by slow, regular changes in the geometry of Earth's orbit and the tilt of its axis.

[■] As the planet's tilt and path around the Sun vary, the amount of sunlight reaching high latitudes in summer rises and falls. When summers stay cool, winter snow survives instead of melting, and over centuries it compresses into ice. [■]

This buried record allows scientists to read past temperatures from the chemistry of trapped air. [■] Cores drilled from thick ice contain bubbles of ancient atmosphere that preserve the gases present when the snow fell. [■] By comparing these layers, researchers reconstruct climate cycles stretching back hundreds of thousands of years. However, the orbital changes alone are too small to explain the full temperature swings, so feedbacks such as shifting ice cover may amplify them, and the complete mechanism remains debated.

questions.stem: The word "uniformly" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: consistently
options.B: briefly
options.C: suddenly
options.D: naturally

questions.explanation: The climate is said not to stay "uniformly cold" but to alternate between phases, so "uniformly" means consistently or evenly.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what allows winter snow to build into ice?

options.A: A steady rise in winter precipitation
options.B: Summers that remain cool enough to prevent melting
options.C: A permanent increase in high-latitude sunlight
options.D: Warmer oceans releasing moisture inland

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "when summers stay cool, winter snow survives instead of melting" and compresses into ice.

questions.stem: The phrase "this buried record" in paragraph 3 refers to

options.A: the geometry of Earth's orbit
options.B: the ice formed from compressed snow
options.C: the sunlight reaching high latitudes
options.D: the milder phases of the climate

questions.explanation: The previous sentence describes snow compressing into ice, which becomes the buried record whose trapped air reveals past temperatures.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about orbital changes?

options.A: They have no measurable effect on climate.
options.B: They fully account for past temperature swings.
options.C: They are insufficient on their own to cause large climate shifts.
options.D: They were discovered through ice cores alone.

questions.explanation: The passage notes orbital changes "alone are too small to explain the full temperature swings," implying other factors must contribute.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'Each annual layer thus seals away a tiny sample of the climate at the moment it formed.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The sentence summarizes how bubbles preserve ancient atmosphere and leads into comparing layers, fitting at slot 4 after the description of trapped gases.

items.passage: A Roman aqueduct is a system of channels and bridges built to carry fresh water from distant sources into cities by means of a steady, gentle slope. The engineers relied on gravity alone, designing each line to fall just slightly over its entire length so that water flowed continuously without pumps. Surveyors measured these gradients with great care, since too steep a drop would erode the channel and too shallow a one would let the water stagnate. Most of the conduit ran underground, following the contours of the land to stay level and protected. Where a valley interrupted the route, builders raised the famous arched bridges that still stand today, carrying the channel high above the ground. These structures were costly, yet they preserved the precise slope across terrain that no buried pipe could cross. Once the water arrived, it fed public fountains, baths, and private homes, transforming daily urban life. The reliable supply supported larger populations than local wells ever could. However, maintaining such networks demanded constant labor and skilled oversight, and when later authorities could no longer fund repairs, many aqueducts gradually fell into disuse and decay.
paragraphs: A Roman aqueduct is a system of channels and bridges built to carry fresh water from distant sources into cities by means of a steady, gentle slope. The engineers relied on gravity alone, designing each line to fall just slightly over its entire length so that water flowed continuously without pumps. Surveyors measured these gradients with great care, since too steep a drop would erode the channel and too shallow a one would let the water stagnate.

Most of the conduit ran underground, following the contours of the land to stay level and protected. Where a valley interrupted the route, builders raised the famous arched bridges that still stand today, carrying the channel high above the ground. These structures were costly, yet they preserved the precise slope across terrain that no buried pipe could cross.

Once the water arrived, it fed public fountains, baths, and private homes, transforming daily urban life. The reliable supply supported larger populations than local wells ever could. However, maintaining such networks demanded constant labor and skilled oversight, and when later authorities could no longer fund repairs, many aqueducts gradually fell into disuse and decay.

questions.stem: The word "stagnate" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: overflow
options.B: evaporate
options.C: sit motionless
options.D: freeze

questions.explanation: A too-shallow slope would stop the water from flowing continuously, so "stagnate" means to sit still without moving.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why did surveyors measure gradients so carefully?

options.A: Because pumps could not be installed underground
options.B: Because an incorrect slope could damage the channel or halt the flow
options.C: Because steeper slopes were always cheaper to build
options.D: Because the water source was constantly changing position

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says too steep a drop "would erode the channel" and too shallow one "would let the water stagnate," so accuracy prevented both problems.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the arched bridges?

options.A: They were built only where the terrain made buried channels impractical.
options.B: They were cheaper to construct than underground conduits.
options.C: They carried water faster than the underground sections.
options.D: They were the most common part of the aqueduct system.

questions.explanation: Bridges appear "where a valley interrupted the route" and crossed terrain "no buried pipe could cross," implying they were used only when burial was impossible.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention public fountains, baths, and private homes in paragraph 3?

options.A: To show how the water supply reshaped everyday city life
options.B: To argue that baths were the main purpose of aqueducts
options.C: To compare Roman homes with modern ones
options.D: To explain how the channels were cleaned

questions.explanation: The list of destinations supports the claim that the supply was "transforming daily urban life."

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of Roman aqueducts EXCEPT:

options.A: They moved water using gravity rather than pumps.
options.B: They supported larger populations than wells could.
options.C: Much of their length ran beneath the ground.
options.D: They required little maintenance once completed.

questions.explanation: The passage states that maintaining the networks "demanded constant labor and skilled oversight," contradicting option D.

items.passage: Linear perspective is a drawing technique that creates the illusion of depth on a flat surface by directing parallel lines toward a single vanishing point. Before its development, medieval painters represented space in a flatter, symbolic manner. They sized figures according to importance rather than distance, so a saint might tower over a building behind him. Spatial relationships in these earlier works followed meaning, not measurement, and the result looked decorative rather than realistic. During the early fifteenth century, artists in Florence began experimenting with geometry to solve this problem. The architect Filippo Brunelleschi is credited with demonstrating a workable method, showing how lines converging toward a horizon could mimic how the eye actually perceives receding space. His ideas were soon written down and spread among painters as practical rules. Later masters refined the system into a precise mathematical procedure, calculating the exact placement of every edge and tile. With these tools, painters could construct convincing interiors and landscapes that drew the viewer's eye into the scene. The technique became a foundation of Western art for centuries afterward. However, some critics argued that such rigid geometry could make compositions feel mechanical, and later movements would deliberately abandon it.
paragraphs: Linear perspective is a drawing technique that creates the illusion of depth on a flat surface by directing parallel lines toward a single vanishing point. Before its development, medieval painters represented space in a flatter, symbolic manner. They sized figures according to importance rather than distance, so a saint might tower over a building behind him. Spatial relationships in these earlier works followed meaning, not measurement, and the result looked decorative rather than realistic.

During the early fifteenth century, artists in Florence began experimenting with geometry to solve this problem. The architect Filippo Brunelleschi is credited with demonstrating a workable method, showing how lines converging toward a horizon could mimic how the eye actually perceives receding space. His ideas were soon written down and spread among painters as practical rules.

Later masters refined the system into a precise mathematical procedure, calculating the exact placement of every edge and tile. With these tools, painters could construct convincing interiors and landscapes that drew the viewer's eye into the scene. The technique became a foundation of Western art for centuries afterward. However, some critics argued that such rigid geometry could make compositions feel mechanical, and later movements would deliberately abandon it.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: How linear perspective developed from flat medieval painting into a precise system
options.B: Why medieval painters preferred symbolic representations of space
options.C: How Brunelleschi designed buildings in fifteenth-century Florence
options.D: Why later art movements rejected mathematical techniques

questions.explanation: The passage traces perspective from earlier flat methods through Brunelleschi to refined mathematical rules, matching the full scope of option A.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how did medieval painters decide the size of figures?

options.A: By measuring their distance from the viewer
options.B: By following strict geometric rules
options.C: By how important the figures were
options.D: By the size of the surrounding buildings

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states they "sized figures according to importance rather than distance."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It repeats the same examples in greater detail.
options.B: It questions whether the earlier method really existed.
options.C: It lists the disadvantages of the technique described before.
options.D: It introduces the solution to the problem described earlier.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 presents the flatness of medieval space as a problem, and paragraph 2 describes Florentine artists experimenting with geometry "to solve this problem."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about Brunelleschi's method?

options.A: He kept his technique secret from other painters.
options.B: His approach was based on how vision works.
options.C: He rejected the use of geometry in art.
options.D: His method was ignored until centuries later.

questions.explanation: His converging lines were meant to "mimic how the eye actually perceives receding space," implying the method drew on real vision.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what did later masters add to the technique?

options.A: They returned to symbolic medieval styles.
options.B: They removed the vanishing point entirely.
options.C: They limited it to landscape painting only.
options.D: They turned it into an exact mathematical procedure.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says later masters "refined the system into a precise mathematical procedure, calculating the exact placement of every edge and tile."
